title: The Somatic Release of Anger: How to Get It Out of Your Body
dek: *When anger stays trapped in muscle and fascia, it hardens into chronic pain, digestive trouble, and nervous system chaos. This piece walks you through body-based practices that let the charge move through and out, restoring the flow your tissues remember.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Anger held in the body creates chronic tension, inflammation, and nervous system dysregulation that talk therapy alone cannot resolve. Somatic practices like shaking, breathwork, and intentional movement discharge stored activation and complete the stress cycle your body started. Regular physical release paired with herbal support restores regulation and prevents anger from calcifying into chronic pain.

She sat in the meeting, jaw clenched so tight her molars ached, nodding politely while her colleague took credit for her research. Her shoulders crept toward her ears. Her breath went shallow. Under the table, her hands balled into fists so hard her nails left crescents in her palms. She smiled. She said nothing. By evening, her neck had seized into a knot that wouldn't release, and her stomach burned with acid she couldn't name. The anger had nowhere to go, so it went inward, lodging itself in the very architecture of her body. ## Why Anger Gets Stuck in Tissue Your body doesn't distinguish between a saber-toothed tiger and a passive-aggressive email. Both trigger the same ancient cascade: adrenaline floods your bloodstream, your heart rate spikes, your muscles contract in preparation to fight or flee. The NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system details how anger activates the sympathetic branch, priming you for explosive action. But here's the problem. In our contemporary lives, you rarely get to discharge that energy the way your body expects. You can't punch your boss. You can't sprint away from the family dinner table. So the activation cycle starts but never completes. The cortisol and adrenaline linger. The muscular contraction persists. The fascia, that web of connective tissue wrapping every fiber and organ, begins to bind and thicken around the held tension. Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, describes how trauma and unresolved emotion literally organize themselves in posture and gesture. The body holds a somatic memory of every swallowed outburst, every bitten tongue, every moment you went still when you wanted to roar. Over months and years, this creates what she calls "procedural learning" in the nervous system. Your tissues learn to clench. Your diaphragm learns to freeze mid-breath. Your jaw learns to lock. In my own practice, I've watched clients arrive with chronic shoulder pain that no massage could touch, only to have it dissolve after they finally screamed into a pillow or pounded a cushion for three minutes. The pain wasn't mechanical. It was stored rage, waiting for permission to move. ## The Physiology of Trapped Activation When anger gets stuck, your body remains in a low-grade state of threat. Your nervous system can't fully return to baseline because the stress cycle never closed. This is not metaphor. This is measurable physiology. Your muscles stay partially contracted, limiting blood flow and oxygen delivery. Fascia loses its fluid, gliding quality and becomes dense and sticky. Inflammatory markers rise. Digestion slows or stops entirely (your body doesn't prioritize breaking down lunch when it thinks you're fighting for your life). Sleep fragments. Your immune function drops. Gabor Maté writes extensively about how suppressed emotion, particularly anger, contributes to autoimmune conditions, chronic pain syndromes, and even cancer. He points to studies showing that people who habitually repress anger show higher rates of inflammatory disease. The body, denied expression, turns the fire inward. It begins to attack itself. You might notice this as tension headaches that appear every Sunday night before the work week. As TMJ that flares when you visit your parents. As low back pain that has no structural cause but correlates perfectly with periods of stress you're not allowed to name. Across the articles we've published on this site, we've seen hundreds of readers describe the same pattern: anger they couldn't express became pain they couldn't ignore. ## Shaking: The Body's Reset Button Animals shake after a threat passes. Watch a gazelle that's just escaped a lion. Once safe, it will literally tremble and shake for several minutes, discharging the adrenaline and cortisol that flooded its system during the chase. Then it goes back to grazing, nervous system reset, activation complete. Humans have the same mechanism. We've just been socialized out of using it. We're taught that shaking means weakness, that trembling is shameful, that we should "hold it together." So we do. And the energy stays lodged in our tissues. Therapeutic shaking, sometimes called neurogenic tremoring, invites that natural discharge. You can do this lying on your back with your knees bent and feet flat, then slowly letting your knees fall open until your inner thighs begin to tremble. Or standing with knees slightly bent, bouncing gently until the shake moves up through your legs and torso. The key is to allow rather than force. You're not making yourself shake. You're creating the conditions for your nervous system to release what it's been holding. In our experience, five minutes of intentional shaking can discharge tension that's been accumulating for weeks. Your breath deepens. Your face softens. You might yawn or sigh involuntarily as your parasympathetic system finally comes back online. Some people laugh. Some cry. Both are signs of completion, of a cycle finally allowed to close. ## Breath That Moves Stagnation Shallow breathing is both a symptom and a cause of trapped anger. When you're holding rage, your diaphragm can't fully descend. Your exhale shortens. Your ribcage becomes rigid. This creates a feedback loop: restricted breath signals danger to your brain, which maintains the stress response, which further restricts breath. Breaking this cycle requires active, deliberate breathing that moves more air than feels comfortable at first. Try this: stand with feet hip-width apart, arms loose at your sides. Inhale through your nose for a count of four, feeling your belly expand. Then exhale through your mouth with sound, a loud "ha" or "huh" that engages your core and forces the stale air out. Repeat twenty times. You might feel dizzy. You might feel angry. Both are good signs. You're moving stuck energy, and it doesn't always feel pleasant on the way out. The sound component matters. Silent breathing keeps the anger polite. Vocalized breath gives it a shape, a pathway, permission to exist in the air outside your body. Over the years I've seen people crack open chronic anxiety with nothing more than two weeks of daily breath practice. The anger they'd been armoring against finally had somewhere to go that wasn't their own tissues. ## Physical Practices That Complete the Cycle Your body prepared to fight. Let it fight something. Not a person, but a pillow, a punching bag, the earth itself. The movement doesn't have to be elegant. It has to be honest. Get a cushion you don't care about. Kneel in front of it. Raise both fists above your head and bring them down hard, again and again, making whatever sounds want to come. Your jaw might open. Your throat might release a sound you've never heard yourself make. Good. That's the anger finding its voice after years of silence. Do this for two minutes without stopping. Your arms will burn. Your breath will rasp. Your nervous system will finally understand that the threat response can complete. Or try this: go somewhere private and throw rocks into a lake or against a stone wall. The weight in your hand, the rotation of your shoulder, the release and the crack of impact - all of this speaks the body's language of discharge. You can learn more about practices like these in [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) we've assembled. Thomas Moore, in his work on the soul's relationship to emotion, reminds us that anger has intelligence. It knows what boundaries have been violated. It knows what needs to change. But we can only access that intelligence once we've let the body move the activation out. Trying to think your way through rage while it's still locked in your fascia is like trying to have a conversation while being strangled. First, you need to breathe. ## The Role of Sound and Voice Anger is loud. It wants to be heard. When you trap it, you're trapping your own voice, sometimes for decades. Many people discover they've lost access to their full vocal range, that they speak only from the throat, never from the deep belly where power lives. Growling, roaring, shouting into a pillow, even humming at a low frequency - all of these vibrate the vagus nerve and signal safety to your nervous system. The sound doesn't need words. In fact, wordless sound often moves more than articulate speech because it bypasses the cognitive editing that keeps anger "appropriate." Find a place where you won't disturb anyone. A car with the windows up works. A forest. Your own home when no one's there. Open your mouth and let sound come from your gut. Start with a hum, then let it grow. You're not performing. You're releasing. The sound might surprise you. It might scare you a little. That means you're touching something real. ## Herbal and Nutritional Support for a Dysregulated System While herbs can't replace somatic practice, they can support a nervous system that's been running hot for too long. Adaptogens like ashwagandha and holy basil help modulate cortisol. B-vitamins, particularly B12, support neurotransmitter production and can buffer against the depletion chronic stress causes. [Garden of Life Organics B-12 Spray (Methylcobalamin)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00K5NEPJY?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers quick absorption when your digestive system is compromised by stress. GABA, the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter, can help calm an overactive sympathetic response. [Solgar GABA 500 mg (100 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0001OP014?tag=spankyspinola-20) provides a straightforward supplement option, though results vary widely between individuals. You can explore other supportive plants in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), where we detail nervines and bitters that help anger move without turning inward. Magnesium, often depleted by chronic stress, relaxes muscles and supports sleep. Lemon balm and passionflower calm without sedating. Bitters taken before meals help restart digestion that anger has shut down. These aren't fixes. They're supports for a body doing the hard work of release. ## A Five-Minute Practice You Can Do Right Now Here's something you can do this moment, wherever you are: Stand up. Shake out your hands like you're flinging water off them. Now raise your shoulders to your ears and hold for a count of five. Drop them suddenly and sigh out loud. Do this three times. Next, make fists and squeeze as hard as you can for ten seconds. Release and shake your hands out. Repeat twice more. Finally, place your hands on your belly. Take five slow breaths, feeling your hands rise and fall. On each exhale, make a quiet "sss" sound like a slow leak of air. You're signaling your nervous system that the threat has passed, that it's safe to stand down. This won't solve chronic rage. But it will complete one small cycle, and that's how you begin. One discharge at a time, one breath at a time, teaching your body that it's allowed to let go. ## What Happens After Release When you finally let anger move through your body, the aftermath can be disorienting. You might feel exhausted, as if you've run miles. You might feel tender, raw, vulnerable. You might feel nothing at all for a while, a strange flatness as your nervous system recalibrates. All of this is normal. You've been holding tension for so long that its absence feels unfamiliar. Your body needs time to remember what baseline actually feels like. Drink water. Rest. Don't immediately fill the space with new activation. Let the emptiness be. Some people report a clarity that follows release, a sudden knowing of what they actually want or need. The anger wasn't obscuring this knowledge. It was pointing toward it. But you couldn't hear the message while your whole system was screaming. Now, in the quiet, you can listen. This is also when grief often appears. Anger and grief are close neighbors, and releasing one frequently reveals the other. Let it. Cry if you need to. The tears are part of the completion, another form of discharge your body knows how to do. ## Common Questions About Somatic Anger Work ### Will releasing anger make me an angry person? No. Discharging anger actually makes you less reactive. When your body isn't carrying a backlog of unprocessed rage, new provocations don't stack on top of old ones. You can respond to what's actually happening now instead of exploding with the accumulated fury of a thousand previous moments. You become more discerning, not more volatile. ### How often should I do somatic release practices? As often as you need them. Some people benefit from daily shaking or breathwork as a preventive practice. Others need intensive discharge only when they notice tension building. Pay attention to your body's signals: jaw clenching, shallow breath, digestive upset, sleep disruption. These are invitations to move something through before it calcifies. In our experience, regular small releases prevent the need for massive eruptions. ### Can I do this work if I have chronic pain or injury? Yes, but with modification. Work within your body's current capacity. If standing shaking hurts your knees, try it lying down. If pounding a pillow aggravates a shoulder injury, try stomping your feet or pressing your palms hard against a wall. The principle remains the same: give your nervous system a way to complete the activation cycle. Consult with a somatic therapist or trauma-informed bodyworker if you need guidance adapting practices to your specific situation. --- The body doesn't lie, and it doesn't forget. Every swallowed word, every polite silence, every moment you smiled when you wanted to snarl - your tissues remember. But memory isn't destiny. What's been stored can be released. What's been frozen can thaw and flow again. You don't need to become someone who rages at the world. You need to become someone who lets rage move through without destroying you from the inside. There's a difference between acting out anger and allowing your body to discharge activation. One creates harm. The other creates space. The work isn't about indulging every furious impulse. It's about giving your nervous system the completion it's been begging for, so you can finally live in a body that feels like home instead of a battlefield. If you're looking for more context on working with difficult emotions, you'll find additional perspectives at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) and in [more articles in this journal](/articles). For further reading on cultural anger and permission, [Rage Becomes Her - Soraya Chemaly](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1501189557?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a powerful exploration of how women in particular are taught to suppress this essential emotion. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on the somatic release of anger: how to get it out of your body, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
